scenario: Juniper Insurance prepares its Camunda 8.8 release with integration tests that need to cover end-user acceptance, end-to-end behavior, and performance under 1500 transactions per hour. A test architect is configuring the integration suite and weighing how much to automate vs run as human-driven exploratory tests.
question: Which rules do the Camunda 8.8 best practices apply to integration tests for process definitions?

options.text: Run them in a close-to-real-life environment, define clear goals such as acceptance, end-to-end, and load tests, weigh automation effort against the project lifespan, and keep them separated from unit and process tests

Run them in the same in-memory engine used for unit tests, skip explicit goal-setting because process tests already cover behavior, and merge them into the unit suite so a single command produces all signals

Run them against the production cluster during business hours, automate every scenario without exception, and treat exploratory tests as a removed in 8.8 practice no longer recommended for Camunda 8.8 projects

Run them only after a manual sign-off by a business analyst, disable performance and load checks because they are covered by Operate dashboards, and reuse fixtures from the process test suite verbatim

a.text: Correct. The Integration tests section says to run them in a close-to-real-life environment, clearly define their goals (end-user acceptance, complete end-to-end, performance and load), carefully consider how much of scope 3 to automate based on regression frequency and project lifespan, and configure them as dedicated tests separated from unit and process tests.

b.text: Incorrect. Integration tests run in a close-to-real-life environment, not the in-memory test engine, and are explicitly kept separate from unit and process tests.

c.text: Incorrect. The best practice keeps human-driven exploratory tests as part of scope 3 and weighs automation costs case by case rather than automating everything.

d.text: Incorrect. The best practice does not recommend disabling performance or load testing or reusing process-test fixtures verbatim; integration tests are configured as dedicated tests.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 integration tests run in a close-to-real-life environment with explicit goals, weigh automation cost against the project lifespan and regression frequency, may include human-driven exploratory tests, and are kept separate from unit and process tests.
